items.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the ATC system?

choices.text: To prevent collisions involving aircraft operating in the system.
To guarantee the fastest possible flow of traffic regardless of other concerns.
To provide additional services before separation duties are considered.
To relieve pilots of responsibility for complying with regulations.

items.explanation: The order states that preventing collisions is the primary purpose of the ATC system.

items.question_text: Which duty has first priority?

choices.text: Separating aircraft and issuing required safety alerts.
Collecting additional weather reports before working the traffic.
Providing every optional service before handling security matters.
Sequencing flights strictly by filing order regardless of circumstances.

items.explanation: Separation and required safety alerts come first, with judgment used for everything else.

items.question_text: When should automation procedures be preferred?

choices.text: When workload, communications, and equipment capabilities permit.
Only when nonautomation procedures are unavailable.
Whenever they reduce controller-to-controller coordination, even if equipment cannot support them.
Only during low-traffic periods when no radar separation is being used.

items.explanation: Automation is preferred when it is operationally suitable and the workload, communications, and equipment support it.

items.question_text: What makes a civil air ambulance flight eligible for operational priority handling?

choices.text: The pilot verbally identifies the flight as MEDEVAC, or verbally requests priority for an AIR EVAC or HOSP flight.
The flight plan contains HOSP, AIR EVAC, or the letter L, even if the pilot never mentions it on frequency.
Any IFR aircraft that files early and remains on its IFR flight plan to the runway.
Any aircraft carrying medical supplies, whether or not the pilot requests priority.

items.explanation: Operational priority for these flights depends on the pilot's verbal identification or request, not flight plan remarks alone.

items.question_text: What happens to an expedite climb or descent instruction if ATC later changes or restates the altitude without saying expedite again?

choices.text: The expedite instruction is canceled.
The expedite instruction remains in effect until the aircraft reaches the final assigned altitude.
The pilot must decide whether expedite still applies.
The instruction automatically becomes an immediately clearance.

items.explanation: Restating or changing the altitude without repeating expedite cancels the expedite instruction.

items.situation: A conflict is developing, but the aircraft still has a brief window to comply before the situation becomes imminent. You need a faster climb now, and there is time to say why.
items.para_context: Prompt compliance is needed, but the situation has not yet become imminent.

choices.text: Use expedite, and include the reason if time permits.
Use immediately, because it always means the same thing as expedite.
Avoid urgency words entirely and issue only the altitude.
Wait until the situation becomes imminent before giving any urgency instruction.

items.explanation: Expedite is for prompt compliance to avoid the development of an imminent situation; immediately is reserved for when the situation is already imminent.

items.question_text: When must a safety alert be issued?

choices.text: When you are aware an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
Only after the pilot requests terrain or traffic assistance.
Only if the aircraft is already in radar contact with your position.
Only after you verify that no other controller has seen the situation first.

items.explanation: A safety alert is required whenever you recognize unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.

items.question_text: What is the first thing you should determine when a pilot reports an inflight equipment malfunction?

choices.text: The nature of the malfunction and the special handling the pilot wants.
Whether the pilot wants to cancel IFR immediately.
Whether the aircraft can still accept every normal procedure without exception.
Whether maintenance can repair the equipment before landing.

items.explanation: Controllers first determine the nature and extent of the malfunction and the handling desired.

items.question_text: What does a minimum fuel advisory mean?

choices.text: The pilot cannot accept undue delay, but the aircraft has not declared an emergency and does not automatically receive priority.
The aircraft is in distress and must be given emergency priority immediately.
The aircraft must land at the nearest airport whether or not the pilot requests it.
The controller should stop issuing any further clearances until the pilot declares an emergency.

items.explanation: Minimum fuel is an advisory about limited tolerance for delay, not an emergency declaration.

items.question_text: What kinds of conditions should be reported as soon as possible to the appropriate office?

choices.text: Any NAS component problem or flight condition that may adversely affect air safety.
Only events that have already caused an accident or incident.
Only weather observations that the ATIS cannot carry.
Only pilot complaints about service delays.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires prompt reporting of conditions that may adversely affect air safety.

items.question_text: If a second aircraft confirms a reported ground-based NAVAID malfunction, what should you do next?

choices.text: Activate the standby equipment or have the monitor facility activate it.
Continue using the aid normally until three pilots report the same problem.
Permanently shut down the aid and stop all related operations immediately.
Tell the reporting pilot to navigate visually instead of taking any equipment action.

items.explanation: A confirmed malfunction leads to standby activation or monitor-facility action.

items.question_text: What is ATC's role when MARSA is being used?

choices.text: ATC does not invoke or deny MARSA; it provides separation between MARSA aircraft and nonparticipating IFR traffic.
ATC approves each individual use of MARSA before the military may apply it.
ATC assumes separation responsibility among the participating MARSA aircraft.
ATC may invoke MARSA whenever workload makes standard separation difficult.

items.explanation: The military decides when MARSA is used; ATC's job is separating the MARSA operation from nonparticipating IFR traffic.

items.question_text: An FAA facility supports a USAF base exclusively. Which military procedures apply there?

choices.text: USAF procedures apply to all traffic at that base.
FAA civil procedures apply to civil traffic and USAF procedures apply only to military traffic.
USN procedures apply unless the aircraft files IFR.
Controllers may choose either FAA or military procedures case by case.

items.explanation: When a facility supports a designated military airport exclusively, the designated military service's procedures apply to all traffic.

items.question_text: How are formation flights controlled?

choices.text: As a single aircraft, with internal separation the responsibility of the formation flight itself.
As separate aircraft whenever they are inside controlled airspace.
As a single aircraft only after the formation reaches cruise altitude.
As separate aircraft unless every pilot in the formation is military.

items.explanation: Formation flights are controlled as one aircraft, while the flight leader and participating pilots handle separation within the formation.

items.question_text: Before issuing a direct instruction that will change an aircraft's heading, route, speed, or altitude in another controller's area, what must be done?

choices.text: Required coordination must be completed with all affected controllers unless an LOA or directive says otherwise.
Nothing, as long as the aircraft acknowledges immediately.
Only the receiving controller needs to be told after the aircraft is already complying.
The pilot must relay the coordination details to the next facility.

items.explanation: Controllers coordinate first when a control instruction will affect another controller's area.

items.question_text: When may you transfer control of an aircraft?

choices.text: Only after any potential conflict with aircraft for which you are responsible has been eliminated.
As soon as the pilot checks in on the new frequency, even if conflicting traffic remains.
Whenever the aircraft is within 50 miles of the next sector boundary.
Only after the receiving controller issues a new altitude.

items.explanation: Control transfer follows the prescribed conditions and only after you have resolved conflicts for which you remain responsible.

items.question_text: Before issuing a clearance that takes an aircraft through another tower's surface area, what must you do?

choices.text: Coordinate with the appropriate nonapproach control tower on an individual aircraft basis unless an LOA says otherwise.
Tell the pilot to get the transit authorization directly from the tower.
Nothing, because radar advisory service alone authorizes surface-area entry.
Wait until the aircraft is already in the surface area and then advise the tower.

items.explanation: Controllers coordinate first when another tower has responsibility for the surface area.

items.question_text: When should radio communications normally be transferred to the receiving controller?

choices.text: Before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area, unless otherwise coordinated or covered by agreement.
Only after the aircraft has passed the center of the receiving area.
Only after the pilot asks for the next frequency.
After the receiving controller has already issued a control instruction on the old frequency.

items.explanation: Communications normally transfer before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area.

items.question_text: What does STAND BY mean when answering an operational request?

choices.text: The request has been acknowledged, but it has not yet been approved or denied.
The request is approved unless the controller later withdraws it.
The request is denied and may not be asked again.
The pilot should proceed at his or her own discretion.

items.explanation: Stand by is only an acknowledgment that a later response will follow.

items.question_text: For which IFR aircraft does wake turbulence separation continue all the way to touchdown?

choices.text: IFR aircraft that are not making a visual approach and are not maintaining visual separation.
Only arrivals more than 20 miles from the runway.
Arrivals behind heavy jets, but not those behind super aircraft.
None; wake separation ends once the aircraft is inside the final approach fix.

items.explanation: The required wake turbulence separation continues to touchdown unless the IFR aircraft is on a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

items.question_text: What should a wake turbulence cautionary advisory include?

choices.text: The other aircraft's position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.
Only the wake category, because the pilot already knows where the traffic is.
Only the runway and an instruction to use caution.
The full route of flight and planned cruising altitude of both aircraft.

items.explanation: Wake cautionary advisories include the traffic's position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.

items.question_text: When may traffic advisories be omitted?

choices.text: When the aircraft is in Class A airspace or the pilot has requested omission.
Whenever both aircraft are IFR and radar identified.
Whenever the controller expects the aircraft to see each other visually.
Only when the traffic is more than five miles away.

items.explanation: The normal rule is to issue traffic advisories, with the specific exceptions of Class A airspace and pilot-requested omission.

items.question_text: For how long should pilot-reported or tower-observed UAS activity continue to be issued to potentially affected aircraft?

choices.text: At least 15 minutes after the last report.
Until the end of the current ATIS code only.
Only while the reporting aircraft remains on frequency.
Exactly 5 minutes unless a supervisor extends it.

items.explanation: Advisories for reported or observed UAS activity continue for at least 15 minutes.

items.question_text: What information should a bird activity advisory include when known?

choices.text: Position, species or size, course of flight, and altitude.
Only the number of birds and the airport name.
Only the species, because pilots can judge the rest visually.
Position and runway in use, but not course or altitude.

items.explanation: Bird activity advisories use the details that help pilots evaluate the hazard: position, type or size, course, and altitude.

items.question_text: How must transfer of position responsibility be accomplished?

choices.text: In accordance with the Standard Operating Practice for Transfer of Position Responsibility and facility directives each time responsibility changes hands.
Only when two specialists disagree about the traffic picture.
At the outgoing specialist's discretion whenever workload is light.
Only when a radar handoff is involved.

items.explanation: Each transfer of operational responsibility follows the SOP and applicable facility directives.

items.question_text: What is the purpose of a wheels-down check?

choices.text: To remind the pilot to lower the wheels, not to shift responsibility to the controller.
To certify that the landing gear is actually down and locked.
To transfer responsibility for landing gear verification from the pilot to ATC.
To replace the pilot's normal before-landing checklist.

items.explanation: The wheels-down check is only a reminder; it does not put landing-gear responsibility on the controller.

items.question_text: Which kind of condition must be brought to the supervisor or controller-in-charge?

choices.text: Any condition that impacts sector or position operations, such as weather, equipment status, overload, emergencies, special flights, or suspicious activity.
Only runway changes that require a new ATIS code.
Only equipment outages that have already caused a loss of separation.
Only emergency declarations, not developing operational issues.

items.explanation: The order requires supervisors or CICs to be aware of a broad range of conditions affecting operations.

items.question_text: When should a pilot be notified of a possible pilot deviation?

choices.text: When it appears a deviation occurred, workload permitting.
Only after the pilot has landed and parked.
Only if law enforcement has already been contacted.
Only if the pilot first requests the notification for the record.

items.explanation: Possible pilot deviation notification is given when it appears warranted and workload permits.

items.question_text: How should you handle an aircraft that reports it is responding to a TCAS resolution advisory?

choices.text: Do not issue control instructions contrary to the reported RA, and continue appropriate safety alerts and traffic advisories.
Assume every nearby aircraft is also responding to the same RA and stop working them.
Immediately cancel the aircraft's clearance and let the crew sort it out alone.
Issue whatever altitude or heading is needed and let the crew resolve the conflict afterward.

items.explanation: The controller does not issue instructions contrary to the RA being flown and continues working the rest of the traffic picture appropriately.

items.question_text: When may a non-RVSM aircraft be allowed into RVSM airspace?

choices.text: Only if it qualifies as an excepted aircraft and has prior operations supervisor or CIC approval.
Any time traffic is light and the pilot requests the altitude.
Whenever the aircraft can accept radar vectors instead of the filed route.
Only after the receiving sector agrees to take separation responsibility.

items.explanation: Non-RVSM aircraft need both excepted status and prior supervisory approval to operate in RVSM airspace.

items.question_text: When does ATC responsibility for approved separation resume after an aircraft responds to a TAWS alert?

choices.text: When the aircraft returns to its assigned altitude, or the crew reports the maneuver complete and you observe separation reestablished, or the aircraft is on an alternate clearance and you observe separation reestablished.
Only after the aircraft lands and clears the runway.
As soon as the pilot acknowledges the last transmission, even if the maneuver is still in progress.
Only when the supervisor personally authorizes ATC to resume separation responsibility.

items.explanation: Separation responsibility resumes only after one of the specific recovery conditions in the paragraph has been met.

items.question_text: What should be done with a report of possible human trafficking, sometimes called a Blue Lightning event?

choices.text: Ensure the supervisor or controller-in-charge is notified.
Wait for confirmation from law enforcement before telling anyone in the facility.
Record it only in local notes unless an emergency is declared.
Pass it only to the next controller handling the aircraft and no one else.

items.explanation: Possible human trafficking reports are elevated to the supervisor or CIC.

items.question_text: When an en route pilot files directly with the center and the information is kept on a voice recorder instead of a strip, what must also be entered on the initial strip?

choices.text: The sector or position number in box 26 so the recording can be located later if needed.
The pilot's home airport and mechanic contact information.
A note that the flight no longer requires search and rescue protection.
The planned runway assignment at the destination airport.

items.explanation: The strip must show where the recorded information can be found if it is later needed.

items.question_text: What happens to the manual forwarding and time-recording requirement during EAS FDP operation when the computer automatically forwards the data?

choices.text: The computer exchange satisfies the forwarding and time-recording requirement for those automatically forwarded data.
Controllers still have to manually forward the same data and record the time by hand every time.
Forwarding is suspended entirely until EAS FDP operation ends.
Only VFR data may be forwarded automatically; IFR data must remain manual.

items.explanation: During EAS FDP operation, automatic exchange records both the data and the time unless a case requires manual handling.

items.question_text: When should a terminal facility forward VFR flight plan data?

choices.text: Forward departure times when requested by FSS or military operations, and forward other VFR data only if the pilot requests it.
Forward every VFR flight plan automatically to every adjacent facility.
Forward VFR flight plan data only after the aircraft lands.
Forward no VFR flight plan data because ATC does not handle it.

items.explanation: The paragraph limits VFR forwarding to requested departure times and pilot-requested additional data.

items.question_text: What must be forwarded on all DVFR departures from joint-use airports?

choices.text: Departure times to the military operations office.
The complete IFR route to every adjacent center.
A cancellation message to flight service.
An automatic weather summary with the aircraft's departure time.

items.explanation: For joint-use airport DVFR departures, the required item is the departure time to military operations.

items.question_text: A pilot tells you they want to change from IFR to VFR. What should you do?

choices.text: Request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS.
Cancel the IFR flight plan for the pilot immediately and assume the change is complete.
Tell the pilot the change can only be made after landing.
Direct the pilot to remain IFR until another controller approves the change by interphone.

items.explanation: The controller requests that the pilot contact the appropriate flight service station for the change.

items.question_text: What should not be used instead of voice coordination to pass control information?

choices.text: The remarks section of a flight progress strip.
A mandatory radar handoff when covered by agreement.
Computer equipment when it is appropriate for the operation.
Controller-to-controller forwarding inside the facility.

items.explanation: The paragraph specifically forbids using strip remarks in place of voice coordination for control information.

items.question_text: If you manually enter a flight plan and a computer-assigned beacon code was forwarded with it, what must you do?

choices.text: Insert the beacon code in the appropriate field as part of the input message.
Ignore the forwarded code and assign a new one locally every time.
Enter the code only after the aircraft is airborne.
Put the code in the remarks section instead of the beacon field.

items.explanation: The forwarded computer-assigned beacon code is entered as part of the manual computer input.

items.question_text: When an aircraft is part of an approved ALTRV, what information should be forwarded?

choices.text: Only the items needed to identify the flight, update ALTRV approval data, or revise previously given information.
Every normal flight plan item, even if it duplicates the ALTRV approval.
No information at all, because ALTRV flights are handled completely outside ATC.
Only the aircraft type and destination airport.

items.explanation: ALTRV forwarding is limited to the information needed for identification and updates.

items.question_text: If your facility cannot automatically obtain acknowledgment of transferred computer data, what must be verified through Service F?

choices.text: Receipt of the message, plus the aircraft identification, assigned altitude, and departure or coordination fix time.
Only the destination airport and requested cruising altitude.
Only whether the pilot has checked in on the next frequency.
The weather at the receiving center and the preferred arrival runway.

items.explanation: Without automatic acknowledgment, Service F is used to confirm receipt and verify the critical transferred data.

items.question_text: During inter-center computer operation, when must NADIN be used for a proposed flight plan even if it falls within PBCT parameters?

choices.text: When the route beyond the originating ARTCC exceeds 20 elements external to that center's area.
Whenever the route enters only one additional center.
Only when the pilot requests a VFR-on-top clearance.
Whenever the aircraft is climbing above FL 180.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires NADIN forwarding when the route has more than 20 external elements.

items.question_text: When does a revised ETA have to be forwarded as amended data?

choices.text: Only when it differs by more than 3 minutes from the estimate that was previously given.
Any time the ETA changes at all, even by a few seconds.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for the new estimate to be forwarded.
Never; ETA changes are always handled locally and are not forwarded.

items.explanation: ETA amendments are forwarded only when the change exceeds 3 minutes.

items.question_text: Which change from an airborne military aircraft must be forwarded to flight service along with the specific supporting details listed in the paragraph?

choices.text: A change in destination or a change in fuel exhaustion time.
A request for a smoother altitude only.
A routine request for another altimeter setting.
A report of light chop with no flight plan change.

items.explanation: The paragraph calls out IFR plan changes such as destination changes and fuel exhaustion updates.

items.question_text: What lead time is used for proposed departures and en route estimates between domestic U.S. ARTCCs and Canadian ACCs unless a bilateral agreement says otherwise?

choices.text: 30 minutes.
5 minutes.
60 minutes.
Only at the time of transfer of control.

items.explanation: Domestic U.S.-Canada exchange uses a 30-minute lead time unless otherwise agreed.

items.question_text: If the data that would print on a Transport Canada ACC strip exceeds the strip form field size, which format should the U.S. ARTCC use instead?

choices.text: The NADIN II input format.
A plain-language free-text message on Service F.
The domestic FDIO terminal strip format.
No message at all until the Canadian ACC requests a manual relay.

items.explanation: Oversized data revert to NADIN II input format instead of the strip-format layout.

items.question_text: When should NRP remain in the remarks section of the flight plan?

choices.text: When the aircraft is moved because of weather, traffic, or other tactical reasons.
Only when the pilot requests a route change for convenience.
Whenever any route amendment is issued, regardless of the reason.
Only after the aircraft leaves the conterminous United States.

items.explanation: NRP stays in remarks when the change is for tactical ATC reasons, not when the pilot asks for a route change.

items.question_text: How should a hand-printed correction be made on a flight progress strip?

choices.text: Do not erase or overwrite; mark out the old item as directed and write the new information adjacent to it in the same space.
Erase the old data completely so the strip stays clean.
Rewrite the entire strip from scratch for every small correction.
Draw a heavy horizontal line through all altitude information, even when the order says not to.

items.explanation: The strip-correction rules are designed to preserve the history of the change while keeping the current value clear.

items.question_text: How should information be entered on FAA Form 7230-19 en route flight progress strips?

choices.text: In the correspondingly numbered spaces for that information.
In any open space as long as the controller understands it.
Only in the remarks section unless the strip is for a departure.
On an attached note rather than on the strip itself.

items.explanation: The strip format is standardized so the required data go in the numbered spaces assigned to them.

items.question_text: What do ATOP reduced separation flags indicate on an oceanic strip?

choices.text: Whether the flight plan qualifies for specific reduced-separation criteria such as MNT, reduced MNT, distance-based longitudinal separation, or RVSM.
That the aircraft is exempt from all coordination and reporting requirements.
That the flight is operating under visual flight rules.
Only that the aircraft has a military call sign.

items.explanation: ATOP's reduced separation flags show which reduced-separation criteria the flight qualifies for.

items.question_text: When may a facility manager authorize omission or optional use of certain terminal strip spaces?

choices.text: When no misunderstanding will result and the omissions or optional uses are specified in a facility directive.
Whenever the controller on position prefers a simpler strip layout.
Only when all traffic is VFR.
Whenever the receiving facility verbally agrees for that one aircraft.

items.explanation: Authorized omissions are allowed only under controlled conditions defined in a facility directive.

items.question_text: How should equipment interchange flights be identified?

choices.text: Use the operating air carrier's company name in the aircraft identity.
Use the painted name on the aircraft even if another company is operating the flight.
Use the destination airport as the call sign.
Use only the aircraft registration and never a company designator.

items.explanation: Equipment interchange flights use the operating air carrier's company name for identification.

items.question_text: Which aircraft type designator should be used on flight progress data?

choices.text: The approved aircraft type designator from FAA Order 7360.1.
Any local shorthand the sector commonly uses.
Only the manufacturer's informal nickname.
Whatever the pilot says over the radio, even if it is not an approved designator.

items.explanation: Aircraft type entry uses the approved designator system, not informal local shorthand.

items.question_text: How is a solo USAF or USN undergraduate student pilot identified in the flight plan?

choices.text: By adding the suffix Z to the aircraft identification in the flight plan, but not using that suffix on the radio.
By saying student pilot after the call sign on every transmission.
By changing the aircraft equipment suffix to Z on the strip and on the frequency.
By using a special squawk code instead of any flight plan notation.

items.explanation: The Z suffix is a flight plan identifier only; it is not used in air-to-ground communication.

items.question_text: How should an aircraft equipment suffix be changed?

choices.text: By changing the ICAO CNS equipment codes so automation can translate them into the proper suffix.
By typing a different slant suffix directly into the strip regardless of the equipment codes.
By asking the pilot to use a different call sign for the rest of the flight.
By changing only the beacon code and leaving the equipment string alone.

items.explanation: The proper way to change the suffix is to amend the underlying CNS equipment information.

items.question_text: What symbol is used at the clearance limit when holding instructions are included in the aircraft's original clearance?

choices.text: H.
F.
O.
W.

items.explanation: H marks a clearance limit that already includes holding instructions.

items.question_text: Where may locally approved identifiers be used?

choices.text: Only within your facility, not on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.
On any circuit as long as the neighboring facility usually recognizes them.
Only in verbal pilot communications, not on strips.
Only in overseas airspace where FAA abbreviations are not standard.

items.explanation: Locally approved identifiers are limited to internal facility use.

items.question_text: When tower positions are combined, which frequency should not be used for airborne communications?

choices.text: Ground control frequency.
Local control frequency.
ATIS frequency when used to advertise the working frequency.
Any frequency shared by tower and TRACON with the same facility name.

items.explanation: When combining tower positions, airborne communications are not conducted on the ground control frequency.

items.question_text: What must be monitored continuously?

choices.text: Interphones and assigned radio frequencies.
Only the primary local frequency and no interphone lines.
Only frequencies with scheduled IFR traffic.
Only frequencies that have had a transmission in the last five minutes.

items.explanation: Continuous monitoring applies to both interphones and assigned radio frequencies.

items.question_text: Which readback must controllers specifically ensure is made?

choices.text: Hold short instructions, whether part of taxi instructions or a LAHSO clearance.
Every weather advisory in full with no abbreviations.
Every charted altitude on every published procedure.
Every traffic advisory, even when the pilot says traffic in sight.

items.explanation: Hold short instructions receive explicit readback emphasis in the paragraph.

items.question_text: When may a pilot be authorized to interrupt communications guard?

choices.text: When it is necessary, such as for a safety-related company communication with only one operative radio.
Whenever the pilot prefers to monitor a company frequency instead of ATC.
Only after landing clearance has already been issued.
Only if the pilot agrees to cancel IFR first.

items.explanation: Guard interruption may be authorized as needed for safety-related communications problems.

items.question_text: What messages may be transmitted?

choices.text: Only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.
Any message that helps reduce radio silence, even if it is not operational.
Routine company messages whenever frequency congestion is low.
Messages unrelated to operations if the pilot asks politely.

items.explanation: The transmission rule is limited to ATC and air-safety purposes.

items.question_text: Which action is required when false or deceptive controller communications occur?

choices.text: Correct the false information, alert aircraft in the affected area, collect details, and notify the operations supervisor.
Wait for another pilot to confirm the transmission before taking any action.
Change only the frequency in use and keep the incident off the record.
Tell aircraft to ignore all future ATC instructions until a NOTAM is issued.

items.explanation: The response includes correction, broadcast warning, information collection, and supervisory notification.

items.question_text: What must you do if you relay an operational message to an aircraft or operator?

choices.text: Give the source of the message and do not agree to handle such messages on a regular basis.
Restate the message as your own so the pilot does not question the source.
Relay any company message routinely whenever the frequency is quiet.
Avoid relaying official FAA messages because only flight service may do that.

items.explanation: Relays are allowed as needed, but the source must be given and routine handling should not be accepted.

items.question_text: What format is used on initial radio contact with an aircraft?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, ATC unit identification, the message if any, and over if required.
ATC unit identification only, followed by whatever the pilot asks next.
Message first, then aircraft identification only if the pilot sounds confused.
ATC unit identification and over only; the aircraft identification is implied.

items.explanation: Initial contact follows the standard sequence of who you are talking to, who you are, and then the message.

items.question_text: Which aircraft identification may be abbreviated after communications have been established?

choices.text: A U.S. civil aircraft using its registration number, by the prefix and the last three digits or letters.
Any military call sign, as long as the controller and pilot have been talking for several minutes.
Any foreign registration, if the controller finds the full call sign cumbersome.
Any similar sounding call sign, because the pilot has already checked in.

items.explanation: Abbreviation is limited, and several categories such as military, foreign, and similar sounding identifications may not be abbreviated.

items.question_text: Which interphone transmission priority comes immediately after emergency messages?

choices.text: Clearances and control instructions.
VFR movement messages.
Flight plans only.
Progress reports only.

items.explanation: The second priority category is clearances and control instructions.

items.question_text: What words are used to interrupt a lower-priority message when you have a higher-priority interphone message to send?

choices.text: Emergency or control.
Stand by or unable.
Priority or urgent.
Override or break-break.

items.explanation: The order specifically names emergency and control as the interruption words.

items.question_text: When may gate or fix names be substituted for interphone position identification?

choices.text: Only when the substitution is operationally beneficial and fully covered in an LOA or facility directive.
Any time the caller thinks it will be faster.
Only between two positions in the same building, with no further requirements.
Only when neither facility uses numeric position identification.

items.explanation: Alternative interphone identifiers are allowed only under controlled, documented circumstances.

items.question_text: How are interphone messages terminated?

choices.text: With your operating initials.
With the word over every time.
With the receiving facility's position number.
With the current UTC time.

items.explanation: Operating initials are the standard interphone message termination.

items.question_text: When may an en route controller omit the words super or heavy?

choices.text: They may be omitted en route except in the cases specifically listed, such as communications with terminal facilities about those operations.
They may always be omitted once radar contact is established.
They may never be omitted in any en route communication.
They may be omitted only if the pilot requests shorter phraseology.

items.explanation: The paragraph keeps super and heavy mandatory in some en route contexts, even though they may be omitted in others.

items.question_text: What should not be done with similar sounding aircraft identifications?

choices.text: They should not be abbreviated.
They should not be spoken with any emphasis.
They should not be acknowledged on the frequency.
They should not be paired with any warning to the pilots involved.

items.explanation: Similar sounding aircraft identifications are emphasized for clarity, and they are not abbreviated.

items.question_text: Which pronunciation standard should be used for numbers and individual letters?

choices.text: ICAO pronunciation.
Any local pronunciation if the pilot sounds domestic.
FAA-only pronunciation for numbers and ICAO only for letters.
Plain conversational English instead of a standard phonetic system.

items.explanation: Numbers and individual letters use the ICAO pronunciation standard.

items.question_text: How should a flight level be spoken?

choices.text: Say flight level followed by the separate digits.
Say the number in group form without the words flight level.
Say the number as a whole thousands value followed by hundred.
Say altitude and then the last two digits only.

items.explanation: Flight levels are spoken with the words flight level followed by separate digits.

items.question_text: After stating a number in the standard form, when may a controller restate it differently?

choices.text: When needed for clarity, using either group form or single-digit form.
Only if the pilot first requests a second reading.
Never; the standard form may not be restated.
Only if the number is an altitude and not an altimeter setting or frequency.

items.explanation: Controllers may restate numbers differently for clarity after first using the prescribed form.

items.question_text: How should a flight service station be identified?

choices.text: State the name of the station followed by the word radio.
State the station's city followed by flight service center.
State the nearest airport name followed by service.
State only the word radio because the station name is implied.

items.explanation: Flight service station identification uses the station name followed by radio.

items.question_text: How should you reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications?

choices.text: Use the full identification in the reply.
Use the shortest possible abbreviation once communications have been established.
Use only the aircraft type so neither pilot hears the other's number.
Reply only to the lead aircraft until the frequency is less busy.

items.explanation: Similar sounding identifications require full replies to keep the transmissions distinct.

items.question_text: When describing a general aviation or air taxi aircraft in a traffic call, what may be added when it helps identification?

choices.text: The manufacturer's name or color.
The pilot's first name and destination.
Only the call sign, never the aircraft type.
The aircraft's maximum certificated weight.

items.explanation: General aviation and air taxi traffic descriptions may include the manufacturer's name or color when useful.

items.question_text: How should airspace classes be pronounced in pilot-controller communications?

choices.text: Use the ICAO phonetic pronunciation of the letters, and class may be dropped.
Spell out the full regulatory name every time, and never drop class.
Use plain English letter names because phonetics are reserved for call signs.
Use military phonetics instead of ICAO phonetics.

items.explanation: Airspace letters are spoken phonetically, and the word class may be omitted.

items.question_text: How are ATS route letters spoken when describing a route such as R20 or A50?

choices.text: Say the letter or letters phonetically, then the route number in group form.
Say the route number first, then spell the letter with plain English names.
Use only the route number once the aircraft is airborne.
Say the whole route as separate digits with no phonetic letters.

items.explanation: ATS route letters use phonetic pronunciation followed by the number in group form.

items.question_text: How should a NAVAID be stated when it is used as a clearance limit and the type is known?

choices.text: State the NAVAID name followed by the type of NAVAID.
State only the NAVAID identifier because the type is never spoken.
State the NAVAID frequency and omit the name.
State the route number that leads to the NAVAID instead of the NAVAID itself.

items.explanation: Known clearance-limit NAVAIDs are spoken as name plus NAVAID type.

items.question_text: How should an unnamed fix based on a radial and distance be described?

choices.text: State the NAVAID name, the radial or localizer or azimuth, the distance in miles, and the phrase mile fix.
Say passing Victor Twelve or passing J Eleven.
Use only the distance in miles because the NAVAID name is implied.
Use the chart title instead of the actual fix description.

items.explanation: Unnamed fixes are described specifically by NAVAID, radial or localizer or azimuth, distance, and mile fix.

items.question_text: What weather familiarization is expected of controllers?

choices.text: Become familiar with pertinent weather when coming on duty and stay aware of current and forecast weather needed to perform ATC duties.
Rely on pilots to report any weather that matters before acting on it.
Review weather only when ceilings drop below IFR minimums.
Check weather at the start of the shift only if assigned to approach control.

items.explanation: Controllers are expected to arrive familiar with the pertinent weather picture and keep it current.

items.question_text: Which PIREPs deserve emphasis in solicitation and dissemination?

choices.text: Both urgent and routine PIREPs, especially when they provide information important to safety or forecasting.
Only urgent PIREPs; routine PIREPs are never important enough to solicit.
Only braking action reports from transport-category aircraft.
Only pilot reports that confirm the forecast, not those that contradict it.

items.explanation: The paragraph emphasizes both UUA and UA PIREPs because they support safe operations and weather forecasting.

items.question_text: If prevailing visibility is less than 4 miles and the tower and surface observations differ, which value is used for aircraft operations?

choices.text: The lower of the two observations.
Always the tower observation, because it is closer to the controller.
Always the surface observation, because it is official.
The average of the two observations.

items.explanation: When the visibility is below 4 miles, the lower observation governs for aircraft operations.

items.question_text: How may the area of observed or reported weather or chaff be defined for aircraft?

choices.text: By clock position and distance from the aircraft, and or by the width and limits of the area using fixes or direction and distance from fixes.
Only by latitude and longitude regardless of how busy the frequency is.
Only by naming the weather product that showed it.
Only by issuing a vector around it without any description of the affected area.

items.explanation: Weather and chaff areas are described in operationally useful spatial terms such as clock position, distance, width, and fixes.

items.question_text: What kind of weather information may terminal controllers transmit without consulting the weather reporting station?

choices.text: General weather statements without specific values, plus elements derived directly from instruments, pilots, or radar.
Any specific ceiling or visibility value the controller judges to be close enough.
Only altimeter settings, never any other weather information.
No weather information at all unless the controller is also the official observer.

items.explanation: The rule distinguishes between general statements and specific observed values.

items.question_text: When are hazardous inflight weather advisories required?

choices.text: When hazardous weather may impact operations within 150 NM of the sector or area of jurisdiction and aircraft on your frequency could be affected.
Only when the weather is inside the airport traffic pattern.
For every SIGMET nationwide, even if it cannot affect any aircraft on your frequency.
Only after a pilot specifically asks for hazardous weather information.

items.explanation: The advisory requirement is tied to operational impact within the controller's area and on aircraft that could be affected.

items.question_text: What are the approved sources of current altimeter settings?

choices.text: Direct-reading instruments or directly from weather reporting stations.
Pilot reports if they are less than an hour old.
Any nearby ATIS broadcast even if its source is unknown.
Averaging the last two settings received on frequency.

items.explanation: Current altimeter settings must come from approved direct sources.

items.question_text: When an en route controller issues an altimeter setting below the lowest usable flight level, what must also be identified?

choices.text: The source of the altimeter setting.
The runway in use at the destination airport.
The identity of the controller who last worked the aircraft.
Whether the aircraft is using standard or metric altimetry.

items.explanation: En route altimeter settings are issued with identification of the source.

items.question_text: If the altimeter setting is greater than 31.00 inches mercury for an arrival, when should the pilot be told to set 31.00?

choices.text: Until reaching the final approach fix.
As soon as radar contact is established after departure.
Only after the aircraft lands.
Only when the pilot specifically asks about high-pressure procedures.

items.explanation: For arrivals under high barometric pressure procedures, pilots are told to keep 31.00 set until the final approach fix.

items.question_text: What controls the requirement to furnish RVR values for the runway in use?

choices.text: Whether the RVR equipment is operational, regardless of later navigation-aid or visual-aid status.
Whether every associated navigational and visual aid remains fully operational after the first report.
Whether the pilot specifically asks for RVR instead of visibility.
Whether the runway is being used only for departures and not arrivals.

items.explanation: Operational RVR equipment drives the requirement to furnish RVR values.

items.situation: Runway visual range equipment is working on the runway in use. Later, a related visual aid becomes unavailable, and another controller says you can stop issuing RVR.
items.para_context: The runway still has operational RVR equipment for the runway in use.

choices.text: Keep furnishing the RVR values for the runway in use.
Stop issuing RVR immediately because the related visual aid is no longer available.
Replace RVR with only tower visibility for the rest of the shift.
Issue RVR only if the pilot asks for it after taxi begins.

items.explanation: As long as the RVR equipment is operational, the values are still furnished for the runway in use.

items.question_text: When must touchdown RVR be issued for the runway in use?

choices.text: When prevailing visibility is 1 mile or less regardless of the value, or whenever RVR shows a reportable value.
Only when prevailing visibility is below one-half mile and the runway has an ILS.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for RVR instead of visibility.
Only when touchdown, midpoint, and rollout values are all below 2,000 feet.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires touchdown RVR in either of those two trigger cases.

items.question_text: How should a value beyond the reportable range of the RVR equipment be stated?

choices.text: As more than or less than the appropriate minimum or maximum readable value.
By rounding it to the nearest normal reportable RVR number without explanation.
By omitting the runway number and saying the visibility only.
By substituting runway visual estimate instead of RVR terminology.

items.explanation: Values beyond the equipment's reportable range are expressed as more than or less than that limit.

items.question_text: How are ATIS code words assigned over time?

choices.text: Sequentially from Alpha through Zulu and then repeated regardless of a new day, with a restart at Alpha after an interruption of more than 12 hours.
They reset to Alpha at midnight local time every day.
They may be chosen in any order as long as the code word is spoken clearly.
They stay on one code letter until the runway changes.

items.explanation: ATIS uses sequential phonetic letters with the specific restart rule for long interruptions.

items.question_text: When must a new ATIS recording be made because of weather alone?

choices.text: Upon receipt of any new official weather, even if the values did not change.
Only when the new official weather changes the ceiling, visibility, or altimeter.
Only when the tower supervisor decides the change sounds operationally important.
Only when a pilot asks whether the weather has changed.

items.explanation: Any new official weather triggers a new ATIS recording, not just changed values.

items.question_text: Which weather observation remarks must always be included in ATIS when present?

choices.text: Lightning, cumulonimbus clouds, and towering cumulus clouds.
Only temperature and dew point, because pilots can see the cloud forms.
Only surface wind direction and velocity.
Only runway visual range and braking action.

items.explanation: Those specific weather observation remarks are always included in ATIS when present.

items.question_text: What is the basic team concept for an en route or oceanic sector?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility; the team as a whole is responsible for safe and efficient operation of the sector.
The radar position alone is always solely responsible for every team task.
The radar associate may act independently without keeping the radar position aware of handoff actions.
Only the coordinator position is responsible for sector objectives once it is staffed.

items.explanation: The section begins with a team concept: no absolute divisions, shared responsibility, and coordinated task execution.

items.question_text: What is the basic team concept for a terminal radar facility or sector?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility; the team as a whole is responsible for safe and efficient operation of the facility or sector.
Only the radar coordinator is responsible for facility objectives once that position is staffed.
The radar associate may accept automated handoffs without making the radar position aware.
The radar position is responsible only for radios, not separation, when a full team is staffed.

items.explanation: Terminal radar teams use the same shared-responsibility concept described in the section.

items.question_text: When the Tower Coordinator Position is staffed, who retains responsibility for aircraft separation?

choices.text: The Tower Positions retain responsibility for aircraft separation, while the coordinator manages traffic flows.
The Tower Coordinator Position assumes both traffic flow management and aircraft separation.
The Flight Data position becomes responsible for all separation decisions.
Responsibility for separation shifts automatically to the approach control facility.

items.explanation: Staffing the coordinator position does not transfer aircraft separation responsibility away from the tower positions.
